description: COVID-19 forced mass remote work adoption in March 2020. Pro-remote preference surged from ~30% pre-pandemic to ~60%+ at peak, then settled around 55% as hybrid models emerged. Tests forced behavioral change driving opinion shift and norm recalibration.

key_events.description: COVID-19 lockdowns force millions into remote work (March 2020). Forced experience rapidly shifts preferences among knowledge workers.
Tech companies (Twitter, Facebook) announce permanent remote work options. Corporate legitimization of remote work model.
Gallup finds 9 in 10 remote workers want to maintain remote work. Peak pro-remote sentiment as productivity data emerges.
Return-to-office mandates from Apple, Google, Goldman Sachs. Hybrid model emerges as compromise. Some backlash and attrition.
Remote/hybrid stabilizes as new normal. Stanford WFH Research shows ~55% of workers prefer hybrid or fully remote arrangements.

calibration_notes: Tests forced behavioral change as opinion driver. Key patterns: experiential persuasion (doing remote work changed minds faster than arguments), rapid ~30pt swing in 3-4 months, partial regression toward mean as novelty faded and isolation costs emerged, employer-employee power dynamics shaping the settled equilibrium, demographic splits (younger/tech more pro-remote). Gallup, Pew, Stanford WFH Research data used.
